topics.title: Overview
content: Computers are widely used in papermaking and paper machine clothing manufacturing.
Applications include product design, process development, manufacturing, testing, automation and information management.
Internet and WWW technologies transformed information exchange and technical communication.

topics.title: Definitions
content: Expert System: Intelligent software using scientific and empirical knowledge.

topics.title: Computer Aided Design (CAD)
content: CAD systems are used for design and development of PMC products.
Fabric structures can be modeled before manufacturing trials.
Finite Element Analysis (FEA) is commonly used for modeling.
CAD helps reduce development time and cost.
Typical CAD systems include monitor, scanner, plotter, printer and digitizer.

topics.title: Computer Integrated Manufacturing (CIM)
content: CIM integrates manufacturing processes using computers.
PLCs are used for machine control and monitoring.
Benefits include improved quality, energy savings, labor savings and process optimization.
CIM integrates process technology, control technology and knowledge technology.
MIS systems support planning, scheduling and production management.

topics.title: Process Simulation and Testing
content: Simulation programs predict fabric behavior during operation.
Fluid flow simulations are used for drainage studies.
Computerized testing improves accuracy and repeatability.

topics.title: Image Technology
content: Image analysis is used to study fabric structures and surfaces.
Microscopic imaging helps evaluate void volume and smoothness.
Documents and drawings can be digitized and electronically processed.

sections.title: Future of Papermaking and Paper Machine Clothing

topics.title: General Future Trends
content: Paper machine speeds are expected to exceed 10,000 fpm.
Machine widths and automation levels will continue increasing.
Demand for better quality and uniformity will increase.
Recycled fiber usage will continue growing.
Environmental concerns will drive process changes.

topics.title: Future of Paper Machines
content: Gap forming and twin-wire technologies will dominate.
Hydraulic headboxes and dilution control systems will increase.
High consistency forming will gain importance.
Automation and fuzzy logic controls will expand.
Closed draw and fully supported sheet transfer systems will increase.

topics.title: Future of Forming Fabrics
content: Higher stability and fiber support will be required.
Three-layer structures will become more common.
Contaminant resistant fabrics will gain importance due to recycled fibers.
Lower caliper fabrics with improved sheet support will be developed.
Pin seam and easy installation fabrics will become more important.

topics.title: Future of Press Fabrics
content: Improved dewatering efficiency will remain critical.
Shoe press applications will continue expanding.
Multilayer and laminated press fabrics will increase.
High void volume structures will support alkaline papermaking.
Impulse drying research may combine pressing and drying operations.

topics.title: Future of Dryer Fabrics
content: Dryer fabrics with better contamination resistance will be required.
Higher temperature and hydrolysis resistant materials will be developed.
Low caliper monofilament structures will improve drying efficiency.
Single tier dryer systems and closed transfers will continue increasing.
Improved seam design and dimensional stability will be important.

topics.title: Polyester Monofilament Size Equivalency
content: Provides relationship between filament diameter, denier, decitex, yards/lb and meters/kg.
Useful for fabric design and yarn selection.

topics.title: Nylon Monofilament Size Equivalency
content: Provides nylon filament conversion tables for PMC applications.
Supports fabric engineering and material selection.

sections.title: Tension Force Calculations of Dryer Fabrics

topics.title: Dryer Fabric Tension Principles
content: Recommended dryer fabric tension is generally 1.5 to 1.8 kg/cm.
High tension may damage fabrics and rolls.

topics.title: Vector Force Calculations
content: Tension calculations depend on roll geometry and angle.
Stretch roll movement direction affects actual fabric tension.
Over-tension increases significantly at larger wrap angles.

topics.title: Research Organizations
content: Institute of Paper Science and Technology
Pulp and Paper Research Institute of Canada
